comments.text: I have published the paper resolving 865 on arXiv here. Note that the paper may be subject to change, as Stijn has improved the constant (C) to 3 3/8.
I've updated the Lean formalization to match the arXiv paper using Aristotle, it can be found here.

I believe I have a candidate proof of the $k=3$ case (which would resolve the problem), giving the sharp bound $f_3(N)\leq \frac{5}{8}N+O(1)$. The main new ingredient is a folded additive lemma. Roughly, if $B\subseteq \{1,\ldots,m-1\}$ is cyclically weakly sum-free modulo $m$, and if $C$ denotes the set of residues which occur both as a non-wrapped and wrapped pair-sum of two distinct elements of $B$, then $|B|-|C|\leq \frac{m}{4}+O(1)$. Folding $A$ around a central element converts these collisions into empty folded coordinates. This gives the $5/8$ bound after a centering argument; the off-center case uses the Choi, Erdős, and Szemerédi $<2/3$ theorem as a coarse input. I am currently preparing a formalization with Aristotle. GPT-5.5 Pro helped me with developing and stress-testing the proof strategy. I will post the full details once Aristotle is finished.

GPT-5.5 Pro has drafted a short paper here. The Aristotle formalization can be found here. Small note: Aristotle treats the coarse theorem as a hypothesis because it is not formalized in Lean yet. Mathematically, this input is from the CES75 paper (which is peer-reviewed) and is also cited on this page. I’m also working on formalizing the input itself in Lean 4 so the whole argument can eventually be Lean-verified (though this may take a while).
As an update to others: I am in touch with Ricky to share some minor comments on the paper, for improvement; and also checking the proof to hopefully confirm this problem is resolved.
I’ve updated the paper with Stijn’s feedback. The link remains the same.

I've further updated the paper with more of Stijn's feedback, removing the need for the coarse theorem via induction. This makes the Aristotle formalization slightly outdated; I will update it as soon as possible.

So as an update(/review): I have confirmed Ricky's paper, who proves a $\frac 58 N +6\frac 38$ bound in his updated paper. (Actually a previous version was read in detail, where I noticed that the $\frac 58 N +O(1)$ could be improved to approx. $\frac 58 N +3$, and checked the crucial points of the "major revision" only briefly). Since $C=2$ seems out of reach due to the idea of Lemma 2.1 not being able to give $m/4+1$ without drastic additional update, a sharp version seems impossible. Since $C$ already settles this problem, taking $C$ slightly larger gave some room to prevent the more annoying things; so also the critical points were correctly handled as such during the edit in a more efficient way. The version of Lemma 2.1 with a bound $m/4+3$ was even simpler. Thus the answer on this problem with $C=7 \frac 38$ is actually very elegant. The main idea is to consider the elements from $A\cap[1,2h]$ that appear multiple times modulo $h$, where $h$ is a well-chosen value near $N/2.$ For this, the structure in $\frac{\mathbb Z}{h \mathbb Z}$ of these double appearing residues are studied, in two steps; lemma 2.1 and lemma 3.1. Finally, using lemma 3.1 the main result is concluded in section 4; here $2$ cases are considered depending on the choice of $h$ being slightly larger or slightly smaller than $N/2.$
Yes, I agree here: C=2 would require strengthening Lemma 2.1, which does not seem easily plausible. I may still investigate this in the near future to see whether anything could work.

If it is true for large N, it will be for all N (possibly with a slightly larger constant C). Maybe interestingly, for $N=8i+6$, where $i \le 6$, the maximum is $5i+5$, except for $N=14$ when the maximum is $11.$ The latter attained e.g. by the set $\{1,2,4\} \cup [N/2, N]$. For $N=8i$ and $3\le i \le 7$, the construction $[N/8,N/4]\cup[N/2,N]$ is actually the only extremal construction (not containing a subset of the form $\{a,b,c,a+b,b+c,a+c\}$) attaining size $5/8N+2$. So this suggests that likely the answer to the question is yes (and $C$ can be even taken rather small).

I will write the detailed constructions and belief here for all $N$. $\textbf{Conjecture}$ For all N, the answer is $\min \left( N, \lfloor 5/8N+2 \rfloor - 1_{\{N \equiv 5 \pmod 8 \vee N \not \in \{13,21\}\}} \right)$, with the exception of $N=14$, in which case $f_3(N)=\lfloor 5/8N+3 \rfloor=11$. When $N \equiv 0,1,2,3,4,5 \pmod 8$, a construction showing the lower bound is $$[\lfloor N/8 \rfloor,2\lfloor N/8 \rfloor] \cup [\lfloor N/2 \rfloor,2\lfloor N/2 \rfloor]$$ where $[a,b]=\{a,a+1, \ldots, b\}.$ For $N \equiv 6,7 \pmod 8$, take as the construction the restriction of the one for the next 8-fold: $$[\lceil N/8 \rceil,2\lceil N/8 \rceil] \cup [\lfloor N/2 \rfloor+1,N].$$ The unique construction for $N=14$ attaining the (special) bound is (through computer verification) $$\{1, 2, 4, 7, 8, 9, 10, 11, 12, 13, 14\}.$$
